title: Between Id and Ego
medium: projection, Arduino, LCD screen, MaxMSP, bitmaps, found footage, recycled wood
short: a multiple-choice text-based RPG, inspired by video game and retro tech culture.
long:
‘Between Id and Ego’ is a multiple-choice text-based RPG, inspired by video game and retro tech culture. Participants, or ‘players’, engage with an Arduino based LCD console in a surrealist experience where they move through dreamscapes and create their own unique storyline. Inspired by old school games such as Zork, DOOM, and Wolfenstein, the piece invokes the layout of these long-standing titles.

 The tactile representation of older video game consoles such as the Gameboy and the PSP were primary inspirations for this piece. It is also supported by a live reactive audio synth on Max MSP to create the soundscapes, and adds another layer of uniqueness to every playthrough, to ensure every player hears a different melody and sound. The form is crafted using recycled wooden palettes to create a free standing structure that contains the entire system the piece requires to run, displayed for the viewer to see its inner workings. The piece contains 3 main elements, the text, the monochrome display which acts as a second person perspective, and a video projection of the characters POV.

The story follows the player character just on the precipice of waking up but goes through endless loops of drifting in and out of sleep. The dreams narrate sensory and visual experiences, inspired by Italo Calvino’s books, primarily the Cosmicomics, and the surreal worlds and people he describes. I wanted to explore those same themes of descriptive, abstract science fiction and build this literary world the player gets to explore with me.

The audio component was created in Audio Art, an elective I had taken that semester. It had originally begun as a procedurally generating concrete music work orchestrated by me, using Microsoft’s inbuilt GS Wavetable Synth, but as I was exploring what I could do with Max, I decided to combine the two projects together. 

I ended up using 6 different ‘instruments’ to create the ‘live’ orchestra that would react to the game and the player’s decisions accordingly. There are some parameters set such as the chords for the melody in the background, using a regular C major progression. The ambient sounds are all synth instruments, allowing the program to change and modulate every noise, including the birds chirping and the sea. Nothing is prerecorded, so every experience for every new player will be unique.

The installation also uses video sourced from found footage online, dating back from the early 20th century. The idea of taking home movies shot on film, scanning and converting them into a digital format is quite odd, and evokes a nostalgia for no specific subject or time.


One of the first issues I ran into was storage space. I had originally planned on using the Microview’s SRAM to store the image data, but the hex strings were too heavy for the limited to 2KB storage. I ended up having to store the image data in the Flash Memory instead, and used an array command to recall them when they were requested by the Uno running the LCD Screen. I was limited to the monochrome output of the Microview. 

I wanted the pieces to look like elements from older video games, and played into the runic, limited style of pixel art. The Microview contains images from the game in a 2nd person perspective, a little window into the player character. The character transforms into different forms, and I wanted to express these changes in a visual way as well. 

Although the bitmaps are black and white, the images themselves are displayed on a blue and black OLED display. The colours would also get inverted when I’d try to display it on the module, so I had to be aware of these changes when working on the files on my computer. Since the images are also limited to a 64x48 pixel resolution, I had to be very economical about how I used my pixels.
